Figure 4. a) The average size and b) volume fraction of NbC particles in Nb-containing steels. ![](images/38b212221aacfe3b421af6cd60913fa11d48b47730166ec9a5ff6f59673abb09.jpg) Figure 5. EBSD orientation color maps of the Nb-containing steels: a) as-cast 0.02Nb steel, b) aged 0.02Nb steel at 823 K for 3 h, c) as-cast 0.04Nb steel, and d) aged 0.04Nb steel at 823 K for 3 h. Furthermore, the black lines in the EBSD maps are the grain boundaries identified by the minimum misorientation angle of 15°, which were used to distinguish different austenite grains. According to the analysis performed by Channel 5 software, the average size of austenite grain is 18 μm in the as-cast 0.02Nb steel, while it is determined to be 9 μm in the as-cast 0.04Nb steel. Noticeably, the austenite grain size remains almost unchanged after aging treatment at 823 K for 3 h, as depicted in Figure 3b,d. In addition, the average size of ferrite grain is measured as 3.6 μm in the as-cast 0.02Nb steel, while it is determined to be 3.1 μm in the as-cast 0.04Nb steel. To investigate the precipitation behaviors of intragranular κ-carbide in Nb-containing steels, TEM characterization was conducted on the steels in various aging states. presents the Figure 6TEM dark field (DF) images of intragranular κ-carbides in austenite for the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, as well as the corresponding SAED patterns. It can be observed that a large amount of nano-scale κ-carbides are dispersed in the austenite. The coherent relationship between the ordered κ-carbides and austenite is demonstrated by the SAED analysis. In order to clearly count the size of κ-carbides, a portion of the TEM DF image was enlarged and only clear areas were selected to count the κ-carbide size by ImageJ software. For each sample, multiple regions were selected for statistical analysis of κ-carbide size, and finally took the average value. The measured content and size of κ-carbides are shown in . The aging treatment increases the content Figure 7and size of κ-carbides, with a higher aging temperature resulting in a greater amount of κ-carbides. After the aging treatment at 823 K, the volume fraction of κ-carbides in 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels increases from 3.3 and 1.6 vol% in the as-cast state to 27.8 and 26.7 vol%, respectively. Furthermore, the average κ-carbide size in 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels also increases from 2.0 and 1.9 nm to 7.8 and 6.4 nm, respectively. Notably, the κ-carbides in 0.04Nb steels consistently have a smaller content and size compared to those in the 0.02Nb steels. # 3.1.2. Mechanical Properties shows the microhardness evolution of 0.02Nb and Figure 80.04Nb steels. As the aging temperature increases, the microhardness of both 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels gradually increases. For the as-cast and aged 0.02Nb steels at 723, 773, and 823 K for 3 h, the average microhardness values are measured as 406, 440, 455, and 467 HV, respectively, while that of the 0.04Nb steels are measured as 408, 452, 465, and 477 HV, respectively. The microhardness of the 0.04Nb steels consistently surpasses that of the 0.02Nb steels in the same state. The engineering stress–strain curves of the Nb-containing steels in various aging states and the specific mechanical properties are shown in and . As the aging temperature increases, the yield strength and tensile strength of both the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels gradually increase, while their ductility decreases. Following the aging process at 823 K, the 0.02Nb steel exhibits an increase in the yield strength from 880 MPa in the as-cast state to 1041 MPa, accompanied by a decrease in total elongation from 39% to 8%. For the 0.04Nb steel aged at 823 K, it experiences a significant increase in yield strength, rising from 896 MPa in the as-cast state to 1056 MPa. Similarly, this increase in strength is also accompanied by a decrease in total elongation, which decreases from 37% to 7%. Overall, the trend of changes in mechanical properties of 0.02Nb steels and 0.04Nb steels is similar. But, the 0.04Nb steels generally exhibit a slightly higher strength and a poorer plasticity than the 0.02Nb steels. ![](images/de7fd82424cb14e3fd7388da0b78fb26518ee40dc21b8311fa6ea080fb902510.jpg)

Figure 7. a) The average size and b) volume fraction of κ-carbides in the Nb-containing steels. # 3.2. Structure and Properties of Aged Steels for Different Periods # 3.2.1. Microstructure Evolution Based on the aforementioned experimental findings, the aging treatment has a significant influence on the microstructure evolution and mechanical properties of the Nb-containing steels. And, after aging treatment at 723 K, the low-density steels could achieve better comprehensive mechanical properties. In order to comprehensively study the effects of Nb content on the microstructure and mechanical properties of medium-Mn low-density steels, the aging treatments at 723 K for longer periods were further conducted on both the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels. ![](images/18173b8a70b82f090d233170a0db5a46b6b34e7c76b13f056645bedf6299f007.jpg)
bar | Samples | 0.02Nb steels (HV) | 0.04Nb steels (HV) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | as-cast | 406 | 408 | | 723 K/3 h | 440 | 452 | | 773 K/3 h | 455 | 465 | | 823 K/3 h | 467 | 477 |
Figure 8. Microhardness evolution of the Nb-containing steels. shows XRD patterns of the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels Figure 10aged at 723 K for 3, 6, and 9 h. Despite the alteration in aging period, the primary phases in the Nb-containing steels remain ferrite and austenite, without observable new diffraction peak in the XRD patterns. exhibits the SEM images of aged 0.02Nb and Figure 110.04Nb steels at 723 K for 6 and 9 h. As the aging time prolongs, the phase boundaries between ferrite and austenite in the Nb-containing steels become irregular, featuring numerous irregular bulges. But, there is no significant change in the volume fraction of constituent phases in the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels. The ferrite contents in the 0.02Nb steels aged at 723 K for 6 and 9 h are measured as 17.1 and 18.9 vol%, respectively. Similarly, the ferrite contents in the 0.04Nb steels are 17.5 and 18.8 vol% for the same aging period. Additionally, compared to 0.02Nb steels, the size of NbC particles in 0.04Nb steels significantly increases with the prolongation of aging period. shows the TEM DF images of κ-carbides in the aged Figure 120.04Nb steels at 723 K for 6 and 9 h. Following the aging treatments for 6 and 9 h, the volume fraction of κ-carbides increases to 18.7 and 19.5 vol%, respectively. Meanwhile, their average size increases to 7 and 9 nm, respectively. Evidently, prolonging aging treatment time promotes the κ-carbide growth in the Nb-containing steels. # 3.2.2. Figure 11. a,b) SEM images of 0.02Nb and c,d) 0.04Nb steels aged at 723 K for different periods. For both the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, the aging treatment at 723 K for 6 h could result in good comprehensive mechanical properties. The yield strength of the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels is increased to 950 and 1010 MPa, respectively. When the aging period was extended to 9 h, the strength of steels no longer increases, and the plasticity decreases significantly. The total elongation of the aged 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels for 9 h decreases to 8% and 5%, respectively. With the extension of aging time, the strength of 0.04Nb steels is still higher than that of 0.02Nb steels, but its total elongation is lower. ![](images/8363324fac871308021ee66b5cd287243113e38cb71f424810e72883dcf4f923.jpg)

# 4. Discussion # 4.1. Microstructure Evolution of Nb-Containing Steel Through near-rapid solidification and subsequent cooling, the retention of metastable austenite at room temperature has been observed in Figure 1 and 2. To examine the impact of Nb addition on the microstructural evolution, the equilibrium phase diagram of Fe-12Mn-9Al-1.4C-3Cr-xNb (wt%, x = 0–0.05) system was analyzed using Thermo-Calc software with TCFE10 database, as displayed in . Previous studies have shown a strong correlation between the constituent phase in near-rapidly solidified Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel and those at the end of equilibrium solidification.[30] According to Figure 14, the Nb content has limited impact on the temperature at which the liquid-tosolid phase transformation occurs, leading to similar constituent phase content in the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels. Noteworthy, the increase in Nb content is found to refine the austenite grain size, as shown in Figure 5. This could be attributed to more intergranular NbC particles in the 0.04Nb steels, which results in a greater hindrance effect on the austenite grain boundary motion and grain growth. Based on the equilibrium phase diagram shown in Figure 14, the small changes in Nb content have a significant impact on the formation of NbC (MC phase in the diagram). As the Nb content increases from 0 to 0.05 wt%, the precipitation temperature of NbC increases from ≈973 to 1573 K. Due to the high formation temperature of NbC, the NbC particles could pin the movement of the austenite grain boundaries during the transformation of δ-ferrite γ-austenite, hindering the growth of austenite grains. More importantly, the increased Nb content in the low-density steels leads to a decrease in both the content and size of κ-carbides, as shown in Figure 6 and 7. To investigate the impact of Nb element on the κ-carbide precipitation, it is necessary to first take into account the chemical composition evolution of austenite. The chemical compositions of austenite matrix in the as-cast Nb-containing steels were analyzed using EPMA, as shown in and . It can be found that the Figure 15 Table 4Nb element is uniformly dispersed within the austenite grain, with only sporadic Nb enrichment at grain boundaries in the Nb-containing steels. This indicates that the slight variations in ferrite and NbC content have minimal impact on the chemical compositions of austenite in the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, particularly for the crucial constituents of κ-carbide, namely Al and C. ![](images/c50954c6971d62f5fca5a77c73b20253981e08243d2ec33059525ee68a731a84.jpg) Figure 14. Equilibrium phase diagram calculated by Thermo-Calc software for Fe-12Mn-9Al-1.4C-3Cr-xNb (x = 0–0.05) system. Considering the uniform distribution of κ-carbides and Nb element in the austenite (Figure 6 and 15), it is reasonable to infer that Nb atoms are present within the κ-carbides. To investigate the impact of Nb atoms on the formation of κ-carbides, a ![](images/f3de7032331df674a65e474e999bb657599a01ec2fd17e222ef62eceac4aabda.jpg)

$2 \times 2 \times 2$ atomic supercell structure was utilized to model the κ-carbide $( { \mathrm { F e } } _ { 3 } { \mathrm { A l C } } _ { \mathrm { { r } } }$ , E21 structure) for first-principles calculations, as depicted in a. For the inclusion of Nb atoms, two Figure 16different positions were considered: the face-centered position of the cube (corresponding to the Fe position in Fe3AlC), as depicted in Figure 16b, and the corner position of the cube (corresponding to the Al position in Fe3AlC), as shown in Figure 16c. The formation energy of the three κ-carbide structures $( \mathrm { F e } _ { 2 4 } \mathrm { A l } _ { 8 } \mathrm { C } _ { 8 } , \mathrm { F e } _ { 2 3 } \mathrm { N b } \mathrm { A l } _ { 8 } \mathrm { C } _ { 8 } ,$ , and $\mathrm { F e } _ { 2 4 } \mathrm { A l } _ { 7 } \mathrm { N b } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 8 } )$ was calculated using equation: $$ E _ {\mathrm{F}} = E _ {\mathrm{DFT}} - \sum N _ {X} \mu_ {X} \tag {1} $$ where $E _ { \mathrm { F } }$ represents the formation energy, calculated as the total energy $( E _ { \mathrm { D F T } } )$ derived from DFT, minus the sum of the product of the number of atoms of each element $X ( N _ { X } )$ and their respective atomic chemical potentials $( \mu _ { X } )$ . The lower $E _ { \mathrm { F } }$ value indicates greater stability. In this study, the formation energy for the structures of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 2 4 } \mathrm { A l } _ { 8 } \mathrm { C } _ { 8 } , \ \mathrm { F e } _ { 2 3 } \mathrm { N b } \mathrm { A l } _ { 8 } \mathrm { C } _ { 8 } ,$ , and $\mathrm { F e } _ { 2 4 } \mathrm { A l } _ { 7 } \mathrm { N b } { \mathrm { C } } _ { 8 }$ was calculated as 0.891, 2.826, and 1.453 eV, respectively. Remarkably, doping κ-carbide with Nb atoms significantly increases its formation energy. Furthermore, with increasing the aging temperature, the average size of κ-carbides in the 0.02Nb steels is consistently slightly larger than that in the 0.04Nb steels. The diffusion coefficients of Al and C in austenite for the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels were calculated using Thermo-Calc software in conjunction with the TCFE10\_MOBFE5 database within the temperature range of 673–873 K. These diffusion coefficients are depicted in a,b. The presence of Nb atoms slightly decreases the Figure 17diffusion coefficient of C in austenite due to the strong affinity between Nb and C. On the contrary, the diffusion coefficient of Al experienced a slight increase. It has been reported that for the growth and coarsening of κ-carbides, the long-range diffusion of Al atoms had a minimal impact, while the diffusion of C atoms was more significant.[31] Therefore, the slower C diffusion caused by increased Nb content would decelerate the nucleation and growth of κ-carbides. ![](images/5b9c3b0d8ff3b7f8cb181d42853f55e1a2da0cdc547b1d7a85dac7fcea5a4356.jpg)

Figure 17. Calculated diffusion coefficients of a) Al and b) C in austenite of the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels. # 4.2. Strengthening Mechanisms of the Nb-Containing Low-Density Steels The uniaxial tensile tests reveal that enhancing Nb content and employing aging treatment could increase the yield strength, as evidenced by Figure 9 and Table 2. As the austenite accounts for over 80 vol% volume fraction, it plays a dominant role in determining the mechanical properties of low-density steels. Consequently, a quantitative analysis is carried out to assess the strengthening mechanisms in austenite for the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels. The contribution of austenite to the yield strength of steels can be assessed as follows: $$ \sigma_ {a} = \left(\sigma_ {0} + \sigma_ {s} + \sigma_ {g} + \sigma_ {p} + \sigma_ {d}\right) * V _ {a} \tag {2} $$ where $\sigma _ { 0 }$ represents lattice friction stress, $\sigma _ { s } , \sigma _ { g } , \sigma _ { p } ,$ , and $\sigma _ { d }$ represent the contributions of solid solution strengthening, grain boundary strengthening, precipitation strengthening and dislocation strengthening, respectively. In the present work, the increased Nb content and the aging treatments exhibit minimal impact on the constituent phase fraction and its chemical compositions, as depicted in Figure 1 and 2, and Table 4. Although increasing the Nb content facilitates the formation of NbC particles in austenite, the effect on solid solution strengthening is limited, attributed to the relatively small addition content of Nb. As a result, the increased Nb content and aging treatment have little impact on the solid solution strengthening in austenite. According to our previous work, the formation of submicronsized particles and aging treatment would influence the dislocation density within austenite significantly.[32] The dislocation strengthening in austenite of the Nb-containing steels can be estimated by the following equation:[33] $$ \sigma_ {d} = M \alpha G b \sqrt {\rho} \tag {3} $$ where the Taylor factor M, which reflects the grain orientation in a random distribution, is set as 3.06.[34] The material constant α is given as 0.23,[35] the Burgers vector b is 0.26,[34] the shear modulus G for austenite is $7 0 \mathrm { G P a } . ^ { [ 3 4 ] }$ And $\rho$ stands for the dislocation density, which is determined using the Williamson–Hall equation:[36,37] $$ \rho = \frac {2 \sqrt {3} \varepsilon}{d b} \tag {4} $$ which incorporates the microscopic strain ε and the average grain size d. To acquire the value of $\varepsilon ,$ the initial step involves obtaining the half-peak width $\beta$ and the related Bragg angle θ for the XRD diffraction peaks. The value of $\beta$ can be obtained using the equation:[38,39] $$ \beta = 4 \varepsilon \tan \theta + \frac {\mathrm{K} \lambda}{d \cos \theta} \tag {5} $$ Among them, K represents a constant and λ signifies the wavelength of Cu Kα1 ray. Equation (5) could be simplified as: $$ \beta \cos \theta = \frac {\mathrm{K} \lambda}{d} + 4 \varepsilon \sin \theta \tag {6} $$ The value of ε in the formula is calculated from the slope of the linear fitting graph of βcosθ–4sinθ. The corresponding values of θ, β, βcosθ, and 4sinθ for austenite in the 0.02Nb are recorded in , and the values of ε and $\rho$ for austenite are shown in Table 5 . Based on the aforementioned data, the dislocation Table 6strengthening in austenite for the as-cast 0.02Nb steel is calculated to be 113.1 MPa. After aging at 723, 773, and 823 K, the dislocation strengthening of austenite in the 0.02Nb steels is 87.4, 70.8, and 40.2 MPa, respectively. For the austenite in 0.04Nb steels, the dislocation strengthening is 169.8 MPa in the as-cast state. After aging at 723, 773, and 823 K, the dislocation strengthening in austenite is 129.0, 106.1, and 98.9 MPa, respectively. The increased Nb content refines the austenite grain, as evidenced in Figure 5. The Hall–Petch formula was used to calculate the grain boundary strengthening $( \sigma _ { \mathrm { g } } )$ of austenite:[40] $$ \sigma_ {g} = k _ {\gamma} d ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \tag {7} $$ Table 5. where $k _ { \gamma }$ is the Hall–Petch coefficient $( 1 4 . 5 5 \mathrm { { M P a } \bullet \mathrm { { m m } ^ { - 1 / 2 [ 4 1 ] } ) } }$ , and d represents the average grain size of the austenite. As the grain size remains unchanged with the aging treatment, the grain boundary strengthening also remains constant. The grain boundary strengthening for the as-cast 0.02Nb steel is calculated as 90.8 MPa. After aging at 723, 773, and 823 K, the grain boundary strengthening in austenite is 90.6, 89.9, and 88.6 MPa, respectively. For the austenite in 0.04Nb steels, the grain boundary strengthening is 128.4 MPa in the as-cast state. After aging at 723, 773, and 823 K, the grain boundary strengthening in austenite is 127.0, 125.8, and 125.6 MPa, respectively. The precipitation of NbC particles and κ-carbides is greatly influenced by both the Nb content and the aging treatments, as illustrated in Figure 4 and 7. Notably, due to the small size, the nano-scale κ-carbides observed in this study could be sheared by dislocations under plastic deformation.[34,42] The strength enhancement attributed to the shearing of κ-carbides can be quantified by:[42] $$ \sigma_ {p} = \frac {M}{N} \frac {\gamma_ {\mathrm{APB}}}{b} \sqrt {V _ {f}} \left[ \sqrt {\frac {1 2 \gamma_ {\mathrm{APB}} r}{\pi G b ^ {2}}} - \sqrt {V _ {f}} \right] \tag {8} $$ where N represents the number of accumulated dislocations aiding in shearing intragranular κ-carbides, $\gamma _ { \mathrm { A P B } }$ stands for antiphase boundary energy, V represents the content of κ-carbides, and r represents the κ-carbide average size. Considering the small size of intragranular κ-carbides, the values of N and $\gamma _ { \mathrm { A P B } }$ were set as 6 and 350 mJ m2 , respectively.[42] By utilizing Equation (8) along with the data obtained from Figure 7, the precipitation strengthening attributed to the κ-carbides in the as-cast 0.02Nb steel is calculated as 59.4 MPa. After aging at 723, 773, and 823 K, the precipitation strengthening by κ-carbide are 180.6, 230.0, and 274.8 MPa, respectively. For the 0.04Nb steels under the same aging condition, the strengthening contribution from κ-carbide precipitation is estimated as 53.1, 127.4, 156.8, and 230.2 MPa, respectively. Due to the impermeable nature of large-size NbC particles, the strength contribution of these particles can be attributing to the Orowan looping mechanism. It could be assessed using the Ashby–Orowan equation:[43] $$ \sigma_ {\mathrm{oro}} = \left(\frac {0 . 5 3 8 G b f ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{D}\right) \times \left(\ln \frac {D}{2 b}\right) \tag {9} $$ The value of f represents the content of NbC particles, while D denotes their average size. Accordingly, the calculated strength contribution by the precipitation strengthening of NbC particles for the as-cast and aged 0.02Nb steels at 723, 773, and 823 K is 4.9, 4.3, 4.2, and 4.7 MPa, respectively. For the 0.04Nb steels, the precipitation strengthening by NbC particles is 6.2, 4.7, 4.6, and 4.8 MPa, respectively In addition, the strength contribution of ferrite for the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels should also be analyzed in detail. First, considering the little effect of the change in Nb content and aging treatment on the chemical composition of austenite (Table 4) and the volume fraction of ferrite (Figure 2), it can be inferred that the chemical composition of ferrite remains almost unchanged. That is to say, the increase in Nb content has a limited effect on the solid solution strengthening in ferrite. Meanwhile, the similar ferrite grain sizes in different samples indicate that the grain boundary strengthening is almost unaffected by Nb content and aging treatment. In addition, according to the equilibrium phase diagram in Figure 14, the increase of 0.02 wt% Nb content would not lead to significant phase transformation and strengthening phase precipitation in ferrite. For the dislocation strengthening in ferrite of the Nb-containing steels, it can be estimated by Equation (3). The Taylor factor $M _ { \mathrm { F } } ,$ , which reflects the grain orientation in a random distribution, is set as $2 . ^ { [ 4 4 ] }$ The material constant $\alpha _ { \mathrm { F } }$ is given as $0 . 4 , { } ^ { [ 4 4 ] }$ the shear modulus $G _ { \mathrm { F } }$ for ferrite is $7 6 \mathrm { G P a } . ^ { [ 4 4 ] }$ The corresponding values of $\theta , \beta ,$ βcosθ, and 4sinθ for ferrite in the 0.02Nb are recorded in , and the values of ε and $\rho$ Table 7for ferrite are shown in . Considering the volume Table 8fraction of ferrite in steels, the dislocation strengthening in ferrite for the as-cast 0.02Nb steel is calculated to be 79.7 MPa. After aging at 723, 773, and 823 K, the dislocation strengthening of ferrite in the 0.02Nb steels is 84.0, 83.5, and 93.3 MPa, respectively. For the ferrite in 0.04Nb steels, the dislocation strengthening is 84.3 MPa in the as-cast state. It can be seen that the increase in Nb content and aging heat treatment have limited effects on the strength contribution of ferrite. shows the calculated strength contributions from var-Table 9ious reinforcement factors in the austenite of low-density steels. With increasing the aging temperature, the effect of dislocation strengthening is reduced, while the effect of κ-carbide precipitation strengthening increases, exceeding the reduction in dislocation strengthening. Moreover, the aging treatment facilitates the formation of NbC particles in austenite, decreasing the total elongation of steels. It is observed that although the κ-carbide precipitation strengthening effect is more pronounced in the 0.02Nb steels, the effects of dislocation strengthening and grain boundary strengthening are more significant in the 0.04Nb steels. Consequently, the 0.04Nb steels exhibit higher yield strength. Moreover, extended aging treatment at 723 K does not markedly alter the yield strength of Nb-containing steels, as depicted in Figure 13. This can be attributed to the fact that although the precipitation strengthening by κ-carbide intensifies, while the grain boundary strengthening remains relatively unchanged with extended aging and the dislocation strengthening diminishes. Consequently, the extension of aging time has only a minor impact on the strength of Nb-containing steels. The 0.04Nb steel aged at 723 K for 3 h exhibits the most favorable comprehensive mechanical properties. Table 9. # 5. Conclusions This study investigated the effects of Nb content on microstructure evolution and mechanical properties of the medium-Mn Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C-0.02/0.04Nb (wt%) low-density steels. The following are the main findings that can be summarized: 1) Both the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels consisted of ferrite and austenite, with a ferrite content of ≈16 vol%. The increase of Nb content promoted the formation of NbC particles, thereby refining the austenite grain size significantly. 2) The Nb element was found to be detrimental to the formation and growth of κ-carbides, which was attributed to the increased formation energy of κ-carbide by Nb doping. Because of this, the aging treatment at 723–823 K could significantly promote the growth of κ-carbides in Nb-containing steels, but no coarsened intergranular κ-carbides were observed. 3) Compared to the 0.02Nb steels, the addition of 0.04 wt% Nb resulted in the yield strength improvement of ≈71 MPa. Based on the strengthening mechanism analysis, the increase in Nb content enhanced the dislocation strengthening and grain refinement strengthening, while it also reduced the κ-carbide content in the austenite, thereby weakening the precipitation strengthening effect in the steels. 4) With increasing the aging temperature, the dislocation density in the Nb-containing low-density steels decreased, weakening the dislocation strengthening, but the precipitation and growth of κ-carbides enhanced the precipitation strengthening in the steel significantly. For the Nb-containing steels aged at temperatures exceeding 773 K, the precipitation of κ-carbide was the principal strengthening factor. # Acknowledgements This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant nos. 52301058 and 52271034), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant no. 2023M732183), Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF (grant no.
